optionList.content: The nurse promises to return after another 30 minutes to assess the client’s pain.
The nurse asks the client to try sleeping.
The nurse tells the client that the pain is all in his or her head.
The nurse reports the client’s situation to the primary health-care provider.
The nurse promises to return after another 30 minutes to assess the client’s pain.
The nurse asks the client to try sleeping.
The nurse tells the client that the pain is all in his or her head.
The nurse reports the client’s situation to the primary health-care provider.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse administers a prescribed pain medication to a client. When the nurse checks on the client after an hour, the client is still in pain and requests more medication. Which action by the nurse indicates advocacy?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The nurse is an advocate of the client, and should be respectful and considerate. The nurse must help the client in pain.
Option 2:The nurse is ignoring the client’s pain by asking the client to try sleeping. As an advocate, the nurse must be respectful and considerate toward the client.
Option 3:The nurse should support the client and listen to the client’s problem, rather than being judgmental.
Option 4:The nurse’s role as the client’s advocate requires the nurse to communicate the client’s concerns to the right authority. The nurse must notify the primary health-care provider if the medication is not having the desired effect.

Test Taking Tips: The nurse acts as an advocate by safeguarding the rights of the client.

optionList.content: Transcultural nursing
Moral development
Basic needs of the client
Interpersonal relations model
Transcultural nursing
Moral development
Basic needs of the client
Interpersonal relations model

studentQuestionArray.content: What was the subject of Dr. Madeleine Leininger’s research?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Madeleine Leininger is the founder of transcultural nursing. Her theory focuses on the use of knowledge of cultures and nursing to provide culturally congruent and responsible care.
Option 2:Kohlberg and Gilligan developed theories about moral development in men and women respectively.
Option 3:Virginia Henderson researched on the 14 basic needs of the client that must be addressed by nursing care.
Option 4:Hildegard E. Peplau researched on the effect of interpersonal relations for improved mental health.

optionList.content: Advocate for the client.
Persuade the client that the family’s decision is in the client’s best interest.
Speak for the primary health-care provider.
Express an inability to act against the facility’s policy.
Advocate for the client.
Persuade the client that the family’s decision is in the client’s best interest.
Speak for the primary health-care provider.
Express an inability to act against the facility’s policy.

studentQuestionArray.content: What should the nurse do if there is a conflict among the interests of the client, the family, the primary health-care provider, and the facility?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:According to the ANA Code of Ethics for nurses, the nurse’s primary commitment is to the client. The nurse must advocate for the client who may be an individual, a family, a group, or a community.
Option 2:The nurse should not persuade the client to accept the family’s decision. The nurse must respect the client’s decision, even if it is against the nurse’s own values.
Option 3:The nurse should not speak for the primary health-care provider. He or she should provide the client with unbiased information, so that the client can make an informed decision.
Option 4:The nurse must protect the client’s right to make decisions and act on them, even if they clash with the facility’s policy.

optionList.content: The surrogate decision maker makes all health-care decisions for the client after signing the document.
The surrogate decision maker makes all health-care decisions for the client only if the client is not able to do so.
The surrogate decision maker makes all decisions as per the client’s living will.
The surrogate decision maker transfers the rights to another family member.
The surrogate decision maker makes all health-care decisions for the client after signing the document.
The surrogate decision maker makes all health-care decisions for the client only if the client is not able to do so.
The surrogate decision maker makes all decisions as per the client’s living will.
The surrogate decision maker transfers the rights to another family member.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client identifies a family member as the surrogate decision-maker for health care issues by signing a durable power of attorney (DPOA). Which is the right action by the surrogate decision maker?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The surrogate decision maker cannot make health-care decisions when the client is capable of making his or her own decisions.
Option 2:The surrogate decision maker makes all health-care decisions for the client only for the duration when the client is not able to do so. This may occur when the client is unconscious, in a coma, or mentally unstable.
Option 3:A DPOA authorizes the surrogate decision maker to make health-care decisions for the client, not the living will.
Option 4:The surrogate decision maker is bound by the legal document and cannot transfer the rights to another family member.

optionList.content: A client who is jobless and without insurance is provided treatment in the emergency department.
Another person makes health-care decisions for an elderly client who is frail and disoriented.
A nurse with cancer is allowed to work on a fixed schedule at the health-care facility.
A nurse documents the presence of a client’s advance directive in the medical record.
A client who is jobless and without insurance is provided treatment in the emergency department.
Another person makes health-care decisions for an elderly client who is frail and disoriented.
A nurse with cancer is allowed to work on a fixed schedule at the health-care facility.
A nurse documents the presence of a client’s advance directive in the medical record.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which case is an example of the impact of the Americans With Disabilities Act?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The Emergency Medical Treatment and Active Labor Act (EMTALA) requires health-care facilities to provide treatment to all who seek health care in the emergency department. Care is provided irrespective of their ability to pay, legal status, or citizenship status.
Option 2:The Patient Self-Determination Act (PSDA) allows a client to identify another person who can take health-care decisions for the client if the client is unable to do so.
Option 3:The Americans With Disabilities Act protects individuals with disabilities against discrimination. The employer is required to provide reasonable accommodations within the work setting to allow employees with disabilities to perform their jobs.
Option 4:The PSDA requires the nurse to document the presence or absence of an advance directive in the medical record.

optionList.content: Record down a value that is an average of the day’s samples.
Refrain from discussing the matter with anybody.
Report the mistake to the RN.
Refrain from recording any detail for that instance.
Record down a value that is an average of the day’s samples.
Refrain from discussing the matter with anybody.
Report the mistake to the RN.
Refrain from recording any detail for that instance.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nursing assistant (NA) has been asked by the registered nurse (RN) to note the volume of urine for a client’s 24-hour urine sample. The NA unintentionally discards one of the urine sample. What should the NA do in this circumstance?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The NA should not record a value that is an average volume of the day’s samples. This is inaccurate and can result in errors further down the line.
Option 2:Although nobody may know if a sample has been lost, the NA has an obligation to tell the truth. He or she must report the mistake to the RN.
Option 3:The moral principle of veracity requires that the NA be truthful. The NA must report the error to the RN who assigned the task.
Option 4:The nurse must be truthful and report the medical error to the RN who assigned the task.

optionList.content: The client has just been diagnosed with prostate cancer and is considering the treatment options.
The client, a victim of a motor vehicle accident, is in coma.
The elderly client is semiconscious in the recovery stage after the surgery.
The client has signed a living will.
The client has just been diagnosed with prostate cancer and is considering the treatment options.
The client, a victim of a motor vehicle accident, is in coma.
The elderly client is semiconscious in the recovery stage after the surgery.
The client has signed a living will.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which situation allows the durable power of attorney (DPOA) to make health-care decisions for the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The DPOA does not make health-care decisions for the client if he is alert and has just been diagnosed with prostate cancer.
Option 2:The DPOA makes health care decision for the client only if the client is unable to do so. The client is in coma; therefore the DPOA is consulted while providing client care.
Option 3:The elderly client who is semiconscious in the recovery stage after the surgery does not need the services of the DPOA. The client is otherwise alert and capable of making own health-care decisions.
Option 4:A living will is an advance directive that enables the client to document his or her desires for end-of-life care. It does not authorize a DPOA to make health-care decisions.

optionList.content: The client may use folk remedies.
The client may practice healing ceremonies.
The client relates health to the cyclic view of life.
The client may speak about the soul when sick.
The client may use folk remedies.
The client may practice healing ceremonies.
The client relates health to the cyclic view of life.
The client may speak about the soul when sick.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which health-care practice is most commonly associated with Cuban Americans?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Cuban Americans are often Catholics, but their practice is personal. They tend to use folk remedies.
Option 2:The Navajo Native American client may practice healing ceremonies to help restore mental, physical, and spiritual harmony.
Option 3:The Chinese American client may view life in terms of cycles and interrelationships. This cyclic explanation of life is connected to health.
Option 4:The Hispanic American clients may believe it is important to talk about the soul or spirit when ill.

optionList.content: Report the condition to an authority within the health care facility.
Advise the colleague to seek medical help.
Warn the colleague about the consequences of the behavior.
Take legal action against the colleague.
Report the condition to an authority within the health care facility.
Advise the colleague to seek medical help.
Warn the colleague about the consequences of the behavior.
Take legal action against the colleague.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is aware that another nurse on the unit has impaired nursing practice due to chemical dependence. What should the nurse do given the circumstances?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The nurse should report the matter to an appropriate authority within the health care facility. The colleague should receive assistance to regain optimal function.
Option 2:An impaired nurse is unable to perform essential nursing functions due to chemical dependency or mental illness. The nurse must approach a higher authority to help the colleague achieve optimal function.
Option 3:The nurse must be compassionate and not issue warnings to the colleague. The nurse must inform a responsible authority within the health care facility to assist the colleague.
Option 4:The impaired nurse is a threat to clients and may be unable to assess self-competence. Therefore, the nurse must ensure that the impaired nurse receives assistance by reporting the behaviors to appropriate authorities within the employment setting.

Test Taking Tips: The nurse must ensure that the clients are not endangered and the impaired nurse receives help.

optionList.content: Privacy
Autonomy
Beneficence
Confidentiality
Privacy
Autonomy
Beneficence
Confidentiality

studentQuestionArray.content: A client’s friend inquires about the client’s medications and health status. The nurse refuses to disclose information to any other person other than the client’s caregiver. Which ethical principle is the nurse adhering to?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Privacy is the right of the client to withhold any personal information.
Option 2:Autonomy is the client’s right to make a decision about his or her fate.
Option 3:Beneficence is the act of doing good to the client. It results in a positive outcome.
Option 4:The nurse treats the client’s health information with utmost sensitivity. The nurse upholds the principle of confidentiality by not refusing to disclose the client’s health information.

optionList.content: To provide insurance if the nurse is injured while providing care
To provide free health care for the nurse
To provide settlement to the claimant for malpractice
To provide settlement in a lawsuit for being a good Samaritan
To provide insurance if the nurse is injured while providing care
To provide free health care for the nurse
To provide settlement to the claimant for malpractice
To provide settlement in a lawsuit for being a good Samaritan

studentQuestionArray.content: Why should a nurse obtain professional liability insurance?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Professional liability insurance does not provide insurance if the nurse is injured while providing care. It protects the nurse from personal losses in a malpractice lawsuit.
Option 2:Professional liability insurance does not provide free health care for the nurse. It helps the nurse during a malpractice lawsuit.
Option 3:Professional liability insurance provides the nurse with an attorney to defend against a malpractice claim and pays the damages awarded to the claimant by the jury.
Option 4:Professional liability insurance does not provide settlement in a lawsuit for being a good Samaritan. The Good Samaritan Laws protect the nurse for helping others in an emergency.

Test Taking Tips: A lawsuit may be brought against a health-care provider for damages due to the death, injury, or other loss for the person being treated.

optionList.content: Baptism of the infant soon after delivery
Prayers and laying of the hands on the infant
Circumcision of the infant on the eighth day
Blessing of the infant by a priest
Baptism of the infant soon after delivery
Prayers and laying of the hands on the infant
Circumcision of the infant on the eighth day
Blessing of the infant by a priest

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse caring for a client of the Jewish faith who has given birth to a male child. Which religious practice would the nurse expect in this community?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Roman Catholics and Anglicans conduct the baptism of an infant soon after delivery if the infant is stillborn or critically ill.
Option 2:Some Episcopal parishes may practice praying and laying of the hands on the sick for speedy recovery.
Option 3:In Judaism, the male infant is circumcised on the eighth day after birth in a religious ceremony known as a bris.
Option 4:The Jewish faith does not follow blessing of the infant by a priest.

optionList.content: Report the incident to another nurse about the prejudiced nature of care.
Report the incident to the nurse administrator.
Raise the issue to public scrutiny.
Warn the colleague and take legal action.
Report the incident to another nurse about the prejudiced nature of care.
Report the incident to the nurse administrator.
Raise the issue to public scrutiny.
Warn the colleague and take legal action.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse observes that another nurse is showing prejudice while providing care to clients from a lower socioeconomic status. How should the nurse deal with the situation?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The nurse need not report the incident to another nurse as he or she has no authority to take necessary action against the nurse who shows prejudice.
Option 2:The nurse should report the action to a person of authority such as the nurse administrator who has the power to stop the wrongdoing.
Option 3:The nurse should report the incident to the nurse administrator. When the wrongdoing is committed by an organization, then the nurse should bring the issue to the notice of the public.
Option 4:When an organization is involved in the wrongdoing, the nurse may approach the media or take legal recourse. The incident must be brought to the notice of a higher authority when it is committed by a colleague.

optionList.content: When a nurse is dependent on chemicals
When a nurse is unable to carry out moral decisions
When the nurse doubts his or her ethical knowledge
When a nurse is unable to prevent wrong doings
When a nurse is dependent on chemicals
When a nurse is unable to carry out moral decisions
When the nurse doubts his or her ethical knowledge
When a nurse is unable to prevent wrong doings

studentQuestionArray.content: In which scenario does impaired nursing practice most likely occur?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Impaired nursing practice may be a result of a nurse’s dependence on chemicals, or of a mental illness. The impairment is a threat to the client’s well-being.
Option 2:Moral distress is the inability of the nurse to carry out decisions based on their moral values.
Option 3:A nurse may experience moral distress when there is a lack of confidence in his or her ethical knowledge. Impaired nursing occurs when a nurse is unable to perform essential nursing functions due to chemical dependence or mental illness.
Option 4:Moral outrage is the inability of the nurse to prevent others in the health care team from doing wrong or behaving immorally.

optionList.content: The client does not have any spiritual beliefs.
The client does not believe in God.
The client believes in an unidentified supreme being.
The client believes in many gods.
The client does not have any spiritual beliefs.
The client does not believe in God.
The client believes in an unidentified supreme being.
The client believes in many gods.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse learns that his or her client is an atheist. What should the nurse understand?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:An atheist may have spiritual beliefs but does not believe in God.
Option 2:An atheist does not believe in God but may have spiritual beliefs.
Option 3:The client who believes in an unidentified supreme being does not belong to any organized religion. This client may have spiritual beliefs and needs.
Option 4:A polytheistic worships many gods.

optionList.content: Fidelity
Justice
Autonomy
Confidentiality
Fidelity
Justice
Autonomy
Confidentiality

studentQuestionArray.content: Which principle requires the nurse to refrain from discussing a client’s health status in public places?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Fidelity requires the nurse to keep promises, however small or insignificant they may be.
Option 2:Justice requires the nurse to be fair to all clients.
Option 3:Autonomy is the right to make one’s own decisions.
Option 4:Confidentiality requires the nurse to refrain from discussing a client’s health status in public places. The nurse must maintain the privacy and confidentiality of the client.

optionList.content: A community health center offers free immunizations to the children in the area.
A nurse raises the bedside rails when the client is sleeping.
A client undergoes a mastectomy to prevent the spread of cancer.
A client wants to know if the treatment will help to prolong life.
A community health center offers free immunizations to the children in the area.
A nurse raises the bedside rails when the client is sleeping.
A client undergoes a mastectomy to prevent the spread of cancer.
A client wants to know if the treatment will help to prolong life.

studentQuestionArray.content: The principle of nonmaleficence is often violated in the short term to produce greater good in the long-term treatment of a client. Which situation is an example of this statement?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:This is an example of distributive justice. Distributive justice includes equal access to health care for all.
Option 2:The nurse raises the bedside rails when the client is sleeping to ensure that the client is not injured by getting out of bed without help. This is an act of beneficence.
Option 3:The client undergoes a painful surgery and postoperative care , which may seem harmful but the consequence is beneficial to the client.
Option 4:The client is exercising autonomy by wanting to know the benefits of the treatment. This helps the client to make the decision of accepting or refusing the treatment.

Test Taking Tips: Nonmaleficence is the principle of doing no harm to the client intentionally or unintentionally.

optionList.content: Girls develop morally by paying attention to community and relationships.
Children go through a sequence of higher levels of moral reasoning ability.
More than half of an individual’s thinking reflects the stage of moral development in which he or she is.
Some people may skip the stages of Kohlberg’s moral development.
Some people may move backward through Kohlberg’s stages of moral development.
Girls develop morally by paying attention to community and relationships.
Children go through a sequence of higher levels of moral reasoning ability.
More than half of an individual’s thinking reflects the stage of moral development in which he or she is.
Some people may skip the stages of Kohlberg’s moral development.
Some people may move backward through Kohlberg’s stages of moral development.

studentQuestionArray.content: What are the findings of Kohlberg’s research on the stages of moral development? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Gilligan’s research suggests that girls develop morally by paying attention to community and relationships. Kohlberg’s research studied the moral development in males.
Option 2:According to Kohlberg, an individual internalizes his or her morals throughout the lifespan, beginning in childhood.
Option 3:Kohlberg found that more than half of an individual’s thinking reflects the stage of moral development in which he or she is. The remainder reflects the stage he or she is leaving or entering into.
Option 4:There are six stages of Kohlberg’s theory of moral development. It has been found that people never skip the stages but usually move forward.
Option 5:It has been found that people usually move forward through Kohlberg’s stages of moral development except in cases of extreme trauma.

optionList.content: Respect and consider the viewpoints of all concerned.
Arrange for a legal settlement of the matter.
Be willing to engage in a discussion.
Try to see the situation from the other’s point of view.
Remember ethical problems are too complex to reach an agreement.
Respect and consider the viewpoints of all concerned.
Arrange for a legal settlement of the matter.
Be willing to engage in a discussion.
Try to see the situation from the other’s point of view.
Remember ethical problems are too complex to reach an agreement.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is trying to resolve an ethical dilemma in the care of a client. What should the nurse do to reach a good compromise? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:A good compromise occurs when the discussion takes place in an environment of mutual understanding and respect. The nurse should respect and consider the viewpoints of all concerned.
Option 2:The nurse must remember that it is not good to settle issues by force. A compromise helps to preserve the rights of the less powerful party in a disagreement.
Option 3:The nurse should be willing to engage in a discussion. This shows mutual respect and willingness to admit that the other party may be right.
Option 4:The nurse should try to see the situation from the other’s point of view. This makes the nurse more open-minded, more creative, and less judgmental.
Option 5:It is not true that all ethical problems are too complex to reach an agreement. It is possible to achieve integrity-producing compromises which are often necessary in nursing.

Test Taking Tips: Mutual respect leads to a compromise solution that preserves the integrity of all the parties.

studentQuestionArray.content:
Arrange the stages of moral development as described by Kohlberg.                     1.Good boy-nice gin orientation
2.Universal ethical principles orientation
3.Legalistic, social contract orientation
4.Law-and-order orientation
5.Punishment-obedience orientation
6.Personal interest orientation
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales
According to Kohlberg, children develop a moral reasoning ability which progresses in a particular sequence until they reach adulthood. The first stage is “punishment-obedience orientation” stage. In this stage, the child understands that an action is right if it avoids punishment. The second stage is the “personal interest orientation” stage. In this stage, the child understands that an action is right if it satisfies personal needs. The third stage is the “good boy-nice girl orientation” stage. In the third stage, the child begins to understand that an action is good if it pleases others. The fourth stage is “law-and-order orientation” stage. By this time, the individual believes that an action is right if it follows the rules. The fifth stage is “legalistic, social contract orientation” stage. At this stage the right action is decided in terms of individual rights and standards agreed upon by the whole society. The sixth stage is the “universal ethical principles orientation” stage. At this stage, the right action is determined by the individual’s conscience and abstract principles.
